speaker: interviewer
text: --interviewing Joseph Carver in London, England. Joe, you were saying about your brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --going--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Palestine. What about the rest of the family? Had you thought of going as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- we could have gone to Argentina where my mother's sisters had gone. But as my father had all his family in Lodz, we stayed together as a unit. And besides, when my brother left in-- in July 1939, we had no knowledge that war would break out in September. Things were normal. And July was a very hot month in 1939. We went to a little place not far from Lodz called Wisniowa Gora. That was a kind of a holiday resort. And the children who went to the Jewish high school, they had a home there. And other Jewish youth clubs used to be in that vicinity. It was really very, very nice. Breakfast, of course, was something which we didn't like, for the simple reason they tried to give us health food in those days. And we didn't like it, but we had to eat it. So most of us would take the bowl, look around, if there was nobody about empty it in the sand, and with our shoes tried to cover it. Because we didn't like that kind of food they were cooking. It was too thick and lumpy. It was not porridge. It was a kind of a barley, which were-- they're dishing out in the morning, kind of a barley [? soup, ?] which we didn't like. And the bread was a black bread, not what we have here in this country. But when when I say black, it was a kind of a-- not a whole meal bread, something like they only have there in Poland or Russia. It's kind of a special black bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next? Where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there, and of course we moaned because we were paying for these holidays. And then they changed the food. They started giving us white bread. And the bread had to be buttered, and it had to be supervised so that the flies would not go and sit on the bread. And we had honey, and we had marmalade, and we had cheese, and we had buttermilk, and we had fruits. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds quite plentiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after one week. Now, the reason why we were dished out poor food in the first week was the people who were organizing it wanted to get rich on our account. So they gave us the basic necessity, not knowing that word will go to our parents, who would come in [INAUDIBLE] station and say, we want our money back, and the children will go somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything went normal, and we spent there about three weeks. And then we came back. It was about the beginning of August. Still had no idea about war breaking out or any sort of politics.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the Shabbat before the war in Lodz, everything came to a standstill, something which I only have seen when I went to Israel. On the Shabbat, everything comes to a standstill. But there in Poland, that's how it was. People went to synagogue. And you could see that even those who were not believers would go, because either their mother or their father requested it from them that they would go. Nobody would light or strike a match or smoke any cigarettes, although some of them were very heavy smokers, really addicts, but never on the Shabbat. They had an eruv in town so that it was permissible to carry. People would get the [? cholent, ?] their Shabbat meal, from the bakers. They would collect their pot, carry it home, and dish it out. Everybody had the samovar. People who know what the samovar is. It's like a urn, which keeps hot water, constant hot water. It's lit, I think, by a little coal fire, some kind of-- what they use for these-- what they call them-- where they have the picnics, you know, these little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like sort of kerosene?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not kerosene really because kerosene is liquid. These are like small little--

speaker: interviewer
text: [? I don't know. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? --cubed ?] things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, like a stove.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sort of a stove, but a little bit different, where they heat the water. And it keeps it going throughout the Shabbat. It's always hot, especially in the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: But [? why ?] do you remember this Shabbat in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Shabbat was--

speaker: interviewer
text: This particular Shabbat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lots of songs, Shabbat songs, and the prayers. Just like being at a concert-- beautifully sung, beautifully chanted. And the ceremony of the Shabbat, when they cut the bread, the challah bread, the benediction. And then when they were sitting down and they had the first course, and in between the songs, the atmosphere was superb, really something nice, something which is kind of impregnated in your memory, especially when you see it in your childhood. A large family sitting around the Shabbat table, having nice songs and talking about these things that happen in the week and what's going on, what one would like to do in a peaceful atmosphere. Still no inkling about that war was on the doorstep. Going, for instance, to a butcher shop on a normal day in Poland, there was such a variety of meat you could choose. Chickens you could have, turkeys, geese, ducks. It was really a treasure, a kind of [INAUDIBLE] there. You could get anything, everything you wanted. As I said, there was a lot of poverty. But despite that, people had to eat. If they didn't have enough, they were given by neighbors. I mean, where we lived, in our courtyard-- it was like a half-moon shape, these houses, these apartment blocks. Some of they're facing the street. Others were facing the interior.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the poor people usually lived down in the basements or on the mezzanine floor. And those who had nothing were usually told by one or two of the neighbors, who were in charge of the welfare of the block, looking who has got nothing, they would stop us and they say, oh, so and so and so has got nothing. Would you give something? So people used to buy challah bread for them, or they used to buy a small little fish. Or they would say, well, they can have two pounds of meat. When I say two pounds, this is about a kilo. A kilo is about two and  a half pounds really [INAUDIBLE] weight. Joe, I'd like to ask you what you remember  about the outbreak of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: We will come to that. So that's how life was before the war. Regarding health service, people, when they were working in a place in the office or in a factory, like my uncle who was the manufacturer, had to pay for each of the employees' sickness benefit. And the sickness benefit was paid to the government. So if any of the employees became sick or needed medical attention, they would go to a clinic, which was free because they were in that scheme, in that health service scheme. I think the first time I had to go to one of these clinics was when I came back from my holidays. This [? actually ?] was 1938. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went to the clinics. All the doctors there were Poles. And they asked me where I was living, what the conditions were in my home. And they prescribed a medicine, which was given in the pharmacy. And of course, although we had a German-sounding name, this was nothing. I mean, they wouldn't sort of take the name as that being like a Jewish name, like Goldberg or Goldfarden. Schnitzer was a typical-sounding German name. And Lodz had a population of 150,000 ethnic Germans who lived there. They had their own hospital. They had their own school. They had their own high school. And some of the biggest manufacturers in Lodz were ethnic Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Poles, who were very anti-Semitic, disliking Jews, also disliked Germans, but not in a way they would hate Jews. They had more respect for the Germans because they were manufacturers, industrialists, well-educated, and all literate, which the Poles not had much education at all. Those who had, just had the basics.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, August passed. And all the sudden, September the 1st, the war broke out. The first thing we done was we were running to get bread. There were queues, all of a sudden queues, very big queues for bread. Now, one of my aunts had a grocery shop, so we went to her. She locked the shop in the front, and we had to go through her living quarters in order to get to the shop. And she told us that she will keep the shop just open for the family. Anybody can come in and buy whatever they have while she had stocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she had flours. And of course we bought the flour, made our own bread, baked our own bread. We got eggs. We got butter from her. We got oil. We got sugar. We got salt, and other things, which were the bare necessities. Other members of the family also went.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my father saw that, in addition to what we had, we should try to get freshly baked bread as well. This was a necessity. We just couldn't rely on using up our flour to bake bread. We really should go and get bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother, myself, and also my father, we used to stay in the queues to get bread, and we were only allocated one loaf per person. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we came home. And of course, people started hoarding because they knew the war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Water we had in the beginning in our tap. When war broke out, the first and second day we had to go down to the courtyard and put a bucket down in the well to bring the water up. That's the way they usually have in Poland. You have a house, and in each courtyard you have like a little well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in an emergency, you can always put the bucket down and bring it up, and you would have fresh water. And that's what we did. We had to use it for cooking and for sanitation, because the toilets, at the time, were blocked. They were not functioning. Everything came to a standstill.

speaker: interviewee
text: The electricity was on. Yes, electricity was on, and we had gas cooking, so the gas was on. It wasn't bombed at the time. Coal fires we didn't have because we run out of coal. So it was cold. And already in September we were wearing two overcoats, two jackets, because we had nothing to warm ourselves. And each day, we heard the Germans were coming nearer. They were fighting here and fighting there. People still went to synagogue. Religious observance was still observed at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So food was scarce, and people standing in queues. And people who had relatives or friends, and they obtained a little bit of food. Till the Germans marched in. The Germans were in Lodz one week after war was declared.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the two days before they came we were playing in our courtyard. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all of a sudden, a boy who was living below us came shouting, there is money on the railway, plenty of money. Go and get money. And he came, he had his shirt full of bank notes. He took his shirt off and made a bundle and put bank notes in it. And all his pockets were stuffed with Polish bank notes. Now, me and my brother, we heard that. We got a holdall, and we were running to the railway station. And there was pandemonium. They had three wagons from the Polish railways with bank notes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was this money doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the banks, I suppose. It got stuck, and people were robbing, plundering, and we were among them. And we had a holdall. We had all our pockets full, and we came home. We-- we really were full. We couldn't carry anymore. Everything was stuffed. And we poured these big Polish bank notes on our table.

speaker: interviewer
text: Under normal circumstances this was a fortune. But in wartime you never know. So we had this fortune. What could we buy? So my father said, well, if you buy tobacco, cigarettes, and vodka-- because this was under the Polish monopoly. And this was obtainable-- not much, but if you had the money you could buy. So when the Germans marched in two days later, on the third day they declared the Polish zloty useless. So whatever we had left [LAUGHS] was paper. We could make a bonfire with it. Lucky enough, we had the presence of mind, and we bought some cigarettes and tobacco, and we had a good few bottles of this vodka, which we could trade in for anything. So straightaway-- oh, yes. When they marched in-- What do you actually remember of them marching in? Can you tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the streets had to be deserted. Nobody was allowed in the streets. They marched in. And the people who were living on the main street could look down and see the German tanks that moved in and the German motorcycles, the cars. Polish Army never had anything like this. All they had was little horses who were short of a meal. It was nothing like this. They were looking at the Germans like people who have landed from Mars.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about it? [? Do you remember? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew the German Army from before the war. We knew it from Germany. It was nothing to us. What we knew straightaway was that-- that Polish Jews were absolutely despicable to the Germans. They hated Polish Jews because they were Hasidic. They were Orthodox. And they didn't like that. So we knew we would get the brunt of it. Straightaway, they came in the streets. They caught Jews in the middle of the road, shaved off their beards, their sidelocks, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made them pick up their own hair. Some were even asked to eat it, which was really gruesome. But to us it was nothing. We knew about these things. It's the poor Polish Jews who were not used to the atrocities and to the brutalities of the Germans. They remembered the Germans in the First World War when they were gentlemen, so to speak of. There wasn't anything about being anti-Semitic at the time. So they couldn't believe that these Germans were like that-- murderers, sadistic. And Polish Jewish women were taken from the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They had to clean up the snow, clean up the streets, which was done before the war by Poles who were-- who got paid from the council to do the cleaning, the street cleaning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month are you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Here it was done by Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: --talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking about now September 1939, when the Germans marched into Poland, into Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was snow about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, snow fell later. The snow fell after September, October, sort of later October. And of course, the rain. And in Poland, the water cistern get overflowed because they haven't got the drainage, and they have a lot of sand. So there's spillage in the street, sandy and sloshy. Yes, as I said, very, very primitive it was. Now, Jewish shops were confiscated. Before the Germans even had a chance to come and take anything away the Poles were looting. They came into the Jewish shops, and they took everything. And then when the Germans, the ethnic Germans, found out what the Poles were doing, they got hold of the Poles, took away what the Poles have robbed, then came back with the Germans, the German SS units, to try to get the last residue what was left. And of course, what they couldn't find, they used to go with the bayonet and poke it around in the pillowcases to see if anything was hiding there or just stick their bayonets anywhere around to find out if anything-- sometimes they found, and sometimes they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those who didn't, they give them a good beating. Sometimes people were beaten to death, just because they were old or because they couldn't understand what the Germans were saying. These units were very brutal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that's happened in the first beginning. Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they used to take, at random, Jews--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: --10. Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- do you remember seeing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They used to take 10 Jews, hang them in the old town of the-- of Lodz, in the old town. And each one had a little placard in front of him why he was hanged. One would say for being a thief, for being a forger, for stealing food from the Germans, and so on, about 10 of them. Each had a different thing. And when they were hanged, people had to see. People were forced to see it. The Poles, of course, were delighted. They clapped hands, you see. They [? saw that. ?] And we could see at the time that the Poles were no friends of Jews, although some, maybe some, very, very small minority perhaps, showed some kindness to Jewish people. But then the kindness was done through money. Jews paid them, either in money or with gold, to look after them or look after their children or possession or help them with bread. But the people who helped were told by their own relatives, don't give shelter to Jews. Because in the Catholic church, the Catholic Polish clergy preached to their flock not to assist Jews because the penalty for assisting Jews is death. And they didn't wanted to lose Polish lives on account of Jews. They said that this is God's judgment because the Jews killed Christ.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here we have it on authority that Poles, even at that time, which was just the beginning of war, were preaching the hatred against Jews more than the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when I was in Auschwitz, and we were deported from there-- oh, well, not deported really. We-- we were forced to leave, and we came to Gross-Rosen. It was a Polish camp. And the Poles said, ah, here the stinking Jews are coming. Get rid of them. And the Germans said, one to the other, look at that. How these Poles hate the Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In another way, these Germans tried to say to the other Germans, no comparison to us. I mean, they really hate them. That were the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So yes, they hanged 10 Jews. The Polish prison was full. I remember going to one because our neighbor, she had a mentally handicapped boy. He was normal [? or ?] right, but there was something wrong with him. I think it was his look that was a little bit different.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was caught by the Germans in the road. They shaved his beard off. They took his hat away, and they put him in prison. And the old mother went with some food to the prison, and she begged me to come because I could speak German. And I went to that prison, and it was staffed by Polish gendarmes, ethnic Germans, which were called Volksdeutsche. They had a kind of black uniform, but not the insignia of the German SS. They just had the swastikas insignias and like a sort of a [? peaked ?] cap, different than the regular German units. And they were in charge of the prison. They looked through the food, made sure that there was nothing inside, like a screwdriver or anything where they could sort of sabotage anything. They were given the food. In the beginning, they were allowed to eat. But they were beaten about. Some Jews we saw were so badly beaten that you couldn't really recognize their face, and they were still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, when we came back, the old mother, she cried, and she shouted. And she said, that what we have seen, and I've seen it. The boy has seen it. And what will happen to my Yankela? Her son's name was Yankel. And she said, they will kill him. What can we do? What can we do? And she-- she prayed, and she shouted. But I believe in the end he was killed there in that prison. So we were still living in town. Those who were living in good apartments had to leave and had to leave his relatives at the time to make do.
